claims.text: Mike 'Madman' Markham, an untrained amateur electronics tinkerer in Stanberry, Missouri, accidentally discovered an apparent teleportation/time-displacement vortex while building a modified, laser-ignited Jacob's Ladder on his back porch.

evidence.quote:
Mike Markham from stanbury
Missouri was an amateur inventor who
liked to Tinker he lacked formal science
training but he had a natural aptitude
for electronics

his
current project was a Jacob's Ladder

hovering above the device was a
sphere of distorted air

Mike
tossed a sheet metal screw into the
energy field the screw disappeared

claims.rationale: Mike Markham is a real person who was arrested in the late 1990s for stealing transformers in northwest Missouri (the case really did make news, and Art Bell really did interview him). The framing of the discovery — that a CD-laser-ignited Jacob's Ladder spontaneously produced a 'vortex' that swallowed and re-emitted screws — is uncorroborated and inconsistent with known electromagnetism; no reproducible mechanism is proposed. Plausible as folk-tale embellishment of a real arrest.

claims.text: Markham scaled up the device by stealing six industrial-grade transformers in broad daylight from the King City, Missouri power-company substation, was caught after his rig browned out the entire town, and was sentenced to 60 days in jail and 5 years' probation.

evidence.quote:
he knew there were six industrial
grade Transformers just sitting in a
yard doing nothing but getting rusty but
that yard was a substation for the King
City Missouri power company

in broad daylight drove to
the substation loaded up the
Transformers and drove away

for stealing the Transformers and
stealing power Mike Markham got 60 days
in jail and 5 years probation

claims.rationale: The transformer-theft arrest, brownouts in Stanberry, MO, and felony charge are reported in real contemporaneous news (the AP/Kansas City Star ran the 'Missouri man tries to build time machine' wire story circa 1998). Sentence figures match commonly cited accounts. The host even quotes Stanberry police chief Tom Hampton later in the video.

claims.text: Markham's arrest produced a regional newspaper headline ('Kansas City man tries to build time machine on porch') in which a University of Missouri-Kansas City physics chair was quoted saying the underlying physics was 'not total nonsense' and that theoretical physicists work in adjacent areas.

evidence.quote:
Kansas City man man tries to build time
machine on
porch a Missouri worker set out to make
a time machine on his back porch the
contraption he came up with was not
completely off the mark according to
scientists

there are theoretical
physicists working on those areas and
it's not total nonsense according to the
chairman at the physics department at
the University of Missouri at Kansas
City

claims.rationale: The headline and physicist quote match the well-known 1998 wire story (chair quoted was Bruce Liley / equivalent at UMKC, depending on which secondary retelling). The 'not total nonsense' framing is a soft, hedged academic comment compatible with charitable engagement, not endorsement. Substantially accurate as reportage.

dependencies.rationale: The newspaper coverage is the downstream consequence of the arrest described in the prior claim.

claims.text: Late-night paranormal radio host Art Bell elevated Markham's story by interviewing him at length on Coast to Coast AM, drawing on his own electronics fluency to vet Markham's technical claims, and mobilizing millions of listeners — and donors who supplied transformers, property, and money for a larger machine.

evidence.quote:
artbell interviewed Mike
Markham for about an hour and a half on
the Coast to Coast radio show

millions of people listened to that
interview that night and before long
people were calling in with offers

claims.rationale: Art Bell did interview Markham on Coast to Coast AM (multiple episodes archived in the Coast to Coast / artbell.com tape inventory). Bell's electronics background (he was an FCC-licensed engineer and ham operator) is well known and lent the interview unusual technical scrutiny for the genre. The audience-mobilization claim — that listeners donated equipment — is consistent with how the show typically operated and how Markham later described his benefactors.

claims.text: With benefactor support Markham built a successively larger third-generation device — eventually a 15-foot rig drawing 3 million volts and using rotating magnetic fields to produce what he called a 'plasma tornado' — in a warehouse in Overland Park, Kansas.

evidence.quote:
Mike set up
in a warehouse in Overland Park Kansas
and there he built several iterations of
his time machine

this thing
looks like a basically a plasma
tornado

claims.rationale: Markham himself describes the warehouse and rotating-magnet plasma device on Coast to Coast, so the existence of a setup is corroborated by his own taped statements. Whether it actually produced a stable plasma vortex with the claimed power draw — 3 million volts is technically possible only with specialized HV equipment and would be hard to hide on a normal industrial connection — is far less credible. Likely a real but more modest experimental rig with embellished specs.

dependencies.rationale: The warehouse build was made possible by the donors recruited via Art Bell's broadcast.

claims.text: Markham personally walked into his vortex during a test, vanished, and re-materialized two years in the future in Fairfield, Ohio (about 800 miles east of his Overland Park warehouse), with no memory of how he got there.

evidence.quote:
Mike
stood in front of the vortex took a deep
breath and jumped

Fairfield is
800 Mi due east of the warehouse in
Overland Park Kansas

he had jumped
2 years into the future

claims.rationale: This is the central paranormal claim of the story and has no independent evidence: no airline / bus records, no medical record of the resulting amnesia, no witnesses to the disappearance. The Fairfield/800-mile detail is a typical 'verifiable particular' added to fringe-narrative testimony. Almost certainly fabricated/embellished, but the host explicitly invites skepticism and even calls Markham's continued presence on Earth the strongest reason to doubt the story.

claims.text: Markham returned to find his Overland Park warehouse stripped — machine, video footage, and all written documentation gone — leaving open whether the equipment was confiscated by the 'Men in Black' (his preferred reading) or simply liquidated by a landlord who assumed he was dead.

evidence.quote:
the machine all the videos that he took
of each test all his notes every bit of
documentation was all gone

claims.rationale: A convenient narrative beat — the missing-equipment story neatly explains why no physical or video evidence of the apparatus exists today. The mundane landlord-liquidation reading is much more probable than a covert seizure; the host floats both options and doesn't insist on either. As stated, the disappearance of any documentation is itself evidentially suspicious: extraordinary claims should be accompanied by extraordinary preservation efforts.

dependencies.rationale: Only meaningful as a follow-up to the prior claim that Markham had jumped forward in time and then returned.

claims.text: Markham's setup — high voltage and rotating magnetic fields — closely parallels the alleged design of the WWII-era Philadelphia Experiment, suggesting (per Art Bell) a meaningful technical convergence between the two projects.

evidence.quote:
art correctly
pointed out that this is how the
Philadelphia Experiment was done with
high voltage and rotating magnetic
fields

claims.rationale: The Philadelphia Experiment itself is widely regarded by historians and the U.S. Navy as a hoax, originating with Carl Allen / Carlos Allende's letters to Morris K. Jessup. Saying Markham's design 'matches' that hoax is therefore a comparison to fiction, not corroboration. The host treats Art Bell's parallel as supportive rather than as a circular appeal to another fringe story.

claims.text: Time travel is not in principle ruled out by physics: Einstein's general relativity allows time dilation via extreme gravity (e.g. near a black hole), and several theoretical solutions involving rotating cylinders or other large-scale spacetime configurations are mathematically compatible with Einstein's field equations.

evidence.quote:
Einstein proved
time travel was possible with gravity
the higher the gravity the faster time
moves

claims.rationale: True with caveats. Time dilation under gravity is a real, experimentally confirmed prediction of GR (gravitational redshift, GPS clock corrections). Closed-timelike-curve solutions exist (Gödel rotating universe, Tipler cylinders, traversable wormholes with exotic matter), but no known physically realizable construction. The host slightly overstates: Einstein didn't 'prove time travel was possible' — GR permits time dilation and admits CTC solutions whose physical realizability is contested. Loosely correct.

claims.text: The strongest reason to disbelieve Markham's time-travel story is that he is still here in our present and apparently unable or unwilling to use the technology again — anyone with a working future-bound time machine would arguably keep using it.

evidence.quote:
really the main reason to be
skeptical of Mike markham's time travel
story is he's here

claims.rationale: An honest concession from the host that essentially undercuts the central paranormal claim. The 'where are all the time travelers?' argument is a familiar Fermi-paradox-style objection; as folk reasoning it's strong, even if it isn't a formal proof of impossibility. Functions as the host's verdict-section concession that the story is more interesting than credible.

dependencies.rationale: If Markham had really jumped forward in time once, his subsequent inability or refusal to do so again is, as the host concedes, the strongest reason to disbelieve the original jump.

claims.text: The Y Files' editorial approach to fringe topics — neither ridiculing nor endorsing the storyteller — is the reason its paranormal coverage outperforms competitors who approach the same material with a snarky or condescending posture.
entities: work_of_media:this episode of the y files

evidence.quote:
I don't condescend
or ridicule I don't talk down to you and
no matter how Bonkers a topic is I don't
ridicule it

claims.rationale: Pure host self-positioning / brand statement. Not a falsifiable historical claim about the world, so I decline to set a directTruth value. It is a genuine description of the host's stated editorial policy and is consistent with the tone of this episode and others in the catalog.
